_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: File Handling practice 1
questions.prompt: Which mode adds new text without deleting existing text?
questions.explanation: Append mode `a` positions writes at the end. Write mode `w` replaces existing contents, while read mode does not permit writing.

questions.title: File Handling practice 2
choices: To remove the newline around each line
To convert each line to a number
questions.explanation: File iteration keeps the newline character at the end of most lines. `strip()` removes it so `print` does not add an extra blank line.

questions.title: Trace File Handling
questions.code:
with open("courses.txt", "w", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    file.write("CS101\n")
    file.write("MATH120\n")

with open("courses.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
    print(file.read())
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The `with` block owns the open file. When the indented block ends, Python closes it even if an error interrupted the work. The resulting output is 'CS101\nMATH120'.

questions.title: File Handling practice 5
questions.code:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    text = file.read()
print(text)
questions.prompt: Why keep `file.read()` inside the `with` block instead of after it?
choices: The file is closed when the block ends, so reading after it fails
read() can only be called outside the with block
print() must be inside the with block
The file stays open forever either way
questions.explanation: `with` closes the file automatically when the block ends, so calling `file.read()` afterward raises a ValueError about I/O on a closed file. Do the reading inside; the *text* is an ordinary string you can freely `print` afterward. That is exactly what this version does.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Writing can replace real data"?
choices: Check the mode before running file code. Opening an existing file with `"w"` clears it immediately.
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Check the mode before running file code. Opening an existing file with `"w"` clears it immediately.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable File Handling practice?
choices: To convert each line to a number
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Use `with open(...)` to guarantee closing.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of File Handling code?
choices:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    pass
text = file.read()
Specify UTF-8 for text you control.
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Specify UTF-8 for text you control.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common File Handling mistake?
choices: Confirm whether the mode reads, replaces, or appends.
To convert each line to a number

with open("notes.txt") as file:
    pass
text = file.read()
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Confirm whether the mode reads, replaces, or appends.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of File Handling. Which advice is correct?
choices: Expect missing paths and permission errors.
To convert each line to a number
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Expect missing paths and permission errors.

questions.title: File Handling concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about File Handling?
choices:
with open("notes.txt") as file:
    pass
text = file.read()
Process large files one line at a time.
questions.explanation: Open files with a `with` statement so Python closes them automatically. Use mode `"r"` to read, `"w"` to replace a file, and `"a"` to add at the end. That reasoning supports the answer: Process large files one line at a time.
